result:
All five functions are implemented in `problems.py` and pass every spec example plus the edge cases the docstrings call out. `rolling_max` runs in O(n) ŌĆö 2M elements with a 50k window in ~0.77s, with spot-checks confirming correctness against brute force.

Summary of the implementations:

- **`parse_duration`** ŌĆö a single `re.fullmatch` on `(?:(\d+)h)?(?:(\d+)m)?(?:(\d+)s)?` enforces order, rejects negatives/whitespace/garbage/unknown units/duplicates in one shot; a non-`str` input and an all-empty match both raise `ValueError`.
- **`luhn_valid`** ŌĆö strips spaces/hyphens, returns `False` (never raises) for empty or any non-ASCII-digit char (guards against Unicode digits like `┬▓`), then runs the standard doubling checksum. `"0"` is valid, other single digits aren't.
- **`flatten`** ŌĆö recursive walk building a fresh dict; empty nested dicts contribute nothing, non-dict values (incl. lists) kept as-is, input never mutated, custom `sep` respected.
- **`top_k_frequent`** ŌĆö `Counter` + sort by `(-count, word)` for the most-frequent-first, alphabetical tie-break; returns `[]` for `k <= 0` and clamps to all distinct words when `k` is large.
- **`rolling_max`** ŌĆö monotonic-deque of indices for true O(n); raises `ValueError` for `window <= 0` and returns `[]` when `window > len(nums)`.

No throwaway files were left behind (checks ran inline), and no commits were made.
